---
title: "Retraction Pollution in the Scholarly Citation Network: A Complete OpenCitations Depth-2 Study"
format:
  gfm: default
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
  docx:
    number-sections: true
params:
  stats_json: ""
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
s <- jsonlite::fromJSON(params$stats_json)
cm <- function(x) {
  if (is.null(x) || length(x) == 0L || is.na(x[1])) return("NA")
  formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "d", big.mark = ",")
}
f1 <- function(x) formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "f", digits = 1, big.mark = ",")
pct <- function(x) paste0(formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "f", digits = 1), "%")
r <- s$reach
pn <- s$post_notice
rc <- s$reconciliation
ctl <- s$control
seeds_affected_pct <- 100 * pn$seeds_with_post_direct / r$seed_papers
sh <- function(x) paste0(formatC(100 * as.numeric(x), digits = 1, format = "f"), "%")
pv <- function(p) {
  p <- as.numeric(p)
  if (is.na(p)) "NA" else if (p < 0.001) "< 0.001" else
    formatC(p, digits = 3, format = "f")
}
```

## Abstract

**Background.** Retracted articles and articles with expressions of concern can
continue to shape later research after public notice. Their influence may spread
through direct citations and through second-order citation paths in which later
papers cite work that had cited the retracted or concerning paper.

**Objective.** To construct and analyze a complete OpenCitations depth-2 citation
network around Retraction Watch records classified as retractions or expressions
of concern, and to describe how far, and how unevenly, that network reaches.

**Methods.** Retraction Watch records were filtered to retractions and
expressions of concern, collapsed to resolved DOI seed papers, and expanded with
OpenCitations. Depth 0 denotes seed papers, depth 1 denotes direct citers of
seeds, and depth 2 denotes citers of depth-1 papers. Bulk extraction was
reconciled against citation-count totals, and every undercounted parent was
checked with targeted REST retrieval. The study was considered complete only when
every depth-0 and depth-1 parent was processed and failed-parent metadata was
zero. All results are descriptive; no causal effect of notices is estimated.

**Results.** The completed graph contains `r cm(s$depth0_nodes)` seed nodes,
`r cm(s$depth1_nodes)` direct-citer nodes, `r cm(s$depth2_nodes)` depth-2
terminal nodes, and `r cm(s$total_edges)` citation edges
(`r cm(s$total_nodes)` nodes total), exported as compressed GraphML. Seed-level
downstream reach is heavy-tailed and highly concentrated: the median seed had
`r cm(r$reach_median)` unique descendants through depth 2 while the maximum was
`r cm(r$reach_max)`, `r cm(r$zero_reach_n)` seeds (`r pct(r$zero_reach_pct)`) had
zero indexed reach, and the Gini coefficient of reach was
`r formatC(r$reach_gini, digits = 3, format = "f")`. The largest neighborhood
belonged to *Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease with a Mediterranean
Diet*. `r cm(pn$direct_unique_nodes)` unique papers cited a seed after that
seed's notice date, affecting `r cm(pn$seeds_with_post_direct)` seeds.

**Conclusions.** Downstream citation exposure to retracted and concerning papers
is concentrated but large: most seed papers have modest depth-2 reach, while a
small tail remains embedded in very large downstream neighborhoods, and citation
after public notice remains common. These are descriptive network results, not
causal estimates of notice effects.

## Introduction

Retractions and expressions of concern are public warnings about the reliability
of scholarly work. Their practical effect depends partly on whether the affected
work continues to circulate through the literature. A retracted article may be
cited directly after notice, but it may also continue to influence later work
indirectly when papers that cited it become sources for still more papers.

This study treats that problem as a network measurement task. The relevant object
is not a list of retracted papers, but a directed citation graph around those
papers, in which a citation edge points from the citing work to the cited work. A
depth-2 graph captures three layers: the seed paper, its direct citers, and
papers citing those direct citers.

The main methodological risk is incompleteness. If direct citers are only
partially expanded, then all downstream reach estimates are lower bounds. The
present study therefore uses a completion criterion: every depth-0 and depth-1
parent must be processed before making a complete depth-2 claim. Depth-2 nodes
are terminal descendants and are not expanded to depth 3.

## Data Sources

The seed set came from a Retraction Watch snapshot labeled
`r s$rw_snapshot_date`. The study included records classified as retractions or
expressions of concern. The loaded seed file contained `r cm(s$seed_records)`
Retraction Watch records. Of these, `r cm(s$resolved_seed_papers)` DOI-bearing
canonical seed papers entered the completed depth-2 graph,
`r cm(s$duplicate_resolved_seed_records)` records were duplicate DOI records, and
`r cm(s$no_doi_seeds)` records lacked DOI identifiers for this
OpenCitations-only run and were excluded (see Limitations).

The citation graph used OpenCitations only; OpenAlex expansion was not used. The
OpenCitations inputs were an OpenCitations DOI citation-count dump
(`10.6084/m9.figshare.24199089.v6`), the OpenCitations Index CSV dataset
(`10.6084/m9.figshare.24356626.v7`, 165 ZIP parts), and an OpenCitations Meta
OMID mapping (Zenodo record `18980639`). OpenCitations access metadata was
recorded on `r s$oc_access_date`.

## Methods

### Seed Construction

Retraction Watch records were filtered to notice types representing retractions
or expressions of concern. Records resolving to the same DOI seed paper were
collapsed to a single source-paper unit for seed-level metrics, keeping the
earliest available notice date so that post-notice classification counts
citations after the first public warning.

### Graph Definition

The network is directed from citing work to cited work: for an edge `A -> B`,
paper `A` cites paper `B`. Depth 0 is the seed papers, depth 1 is papers directly
citing a seed, and depth 2 is papers citing a depth-1 paper. Node depth is the
shortest distance from any seed; when the same source-target edge appeared at
multiple depths, the stored edge retained the shallowest observed depth.
Consequently, per-seed reach is measured on this globally depth-labeled graph: a
paper two steps from one seed but one step from another is labeled depth 1 and is
counted among the direct citers of the nearer seed, not the depth-2 descendants
of the farther one.

### Completion Procedure

The completed study used a bulk-first OpenCitations workflow: pending depth-1 DOI
parents were joined to the citation-count dump; zero-citation DOI parents
(`r cm(rc$zero_count_pruned)`) and non-DOI parents (`r cm(rc$non_doi_terminal)`)
were marked terminal; the remaining `r cm(rc$nonzero_depth1_parents)` nonzero DOI
parents were mapped to OMIDs; all 165 Index CSV parts were scanned for
citing-cited OMID pairs (`r cm(rc$bulk_extracted_rows)` extracted rows,
`r cm(rc$unique_citing_omids)` unique citing OMIDs, `r cm(rc$unique_citing_omids_mapped)`
mapped to DOI or PMID); bulk edges were inserted into DuckDB; and parents
undercounted by the bulk scan relative to the citation-count dump were reconciled
with targeted REST calls.

The mismatch audit found `r cm(rc$exact_parents)` exact parent counts,
`r cm(rc$undercount_parents)` undercounted parents, and
`r cm(rc$overcount_parents)` overcounted parents. All
`r cm(rc$rest_parents_processed)` undercounted parents were reprocessed by
targeted REST retrieval with `r cm(rc$rest_failures)` failures
(`r cm(rc$rest_rows)` citation rows returned). The overcounted parents were not
re-fetched; because bulk extraction found only `r cm(rc$overcount_extra_citations)`
citations more than the citation-count dump across all overcounted parents
(net delta `r cm(rc$net_delta_citations)` against a graph of
`r cm(s$total_edges)` edges), the maximum possible overcount inflation is under
0.1% of the edge total. After final reconciliation,
`r cm(rc$stale_edges_deleted)` stale out-of-frontier citation rows targeting
`r cm(rc$stale_missing_targets)` non-frontier IDs were removed.

### Analysis

The analysis summarized node counts by depth, edge counts by depth, seed-level
direct and depth-2 reach, post-notice citation activity, and high-volume depth-1
bridge identifiers. Every number and figure below is generated from the DuckDB
store and the analysis tables by committed code (`R/manuscript.R`); no value is
hand-entered. Because the full graph is too large for an in-memory `igraph` path,
the study exports the full induced graph as streaming compressed GraphML rather
than computing force-directed layouts.

## Results

### The Depth-2 Frontier Is Complete

All depth-0 and depth-1 parents were processed; depth 2 is terminal by design.

```{r depth-table}
dc <- s$depth_counts
dc$processed <- c(dc$node_count[dc$depth < 2], 0)[seq_len(nrow(dc))]
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  Depth = dc$depth,
  `Node count` = cm(dc$node_count),
  `Processed` = ifelse(dc$depth < 2, cm(dc$node_count), "0 (terminal)"),
  check.names = FALSE))
```

![**Figure 1. Depth-2 network size by frontier layer.** The graph expands from `r cm(s$depth0_nodes)` seeds to `r cm(s$depth1_nodes)` direct citers and `r cm(s$depth2_nodes)` terminal depth-2 descendants (log y-axis).](figures/manuscript_fig1_depth_counts.png)

### Seed-Level Reach Is Heavy-Tailed and Highly Concentrated

The median resolved seed had `r cm(r$reach_median)` unique descendants through
depth 2; the mean was `r f1(r$reach_mean)` and the maximum `r cm(r$reach_max)`.
The 99th percentile was `r cm(round(r$reach_p99))`. `r cm(r$zero_reach_n)` seeds
(`r pct(r$zero_reach_pct)`) had zero indexed reach, reflecting citation-index
coverage rather than a lack of downstream influence. Reach is extremely
concentrated: the Gini coefficient is
`r formatC(r$reach_gini, digits = 3, format = "f")`.

![**Figure 2. Downstream reach is heavy-tailed (A) and highly concentrated (B).** Panel A is the complementary CDF of seed-level depth-2 reach on log-log axes, with the median, 99th percentile, and maximum marked. Panel B is the Lorenz curve of total reach across seeds; the diagonal is perfect equality.](figures/manuscript_fig2_reach_survival_concentration.png)

The largest neighborhoods are driven mainly by depth-2 descendants rather than by
direct citers alone, which is why the study needed a complete depth-2 graph
rather than a direct-citation analysis.

```{r top-seeds}
t <- s$top10
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  Rank = seq_len(nrow(t)),
  Title = substr(t$title, 1, 60),
  `Notice` = t$notice_type,
  `Direct` = cm(t$direct_citers),
  `Depth-2` = cm(t$depth2_descendants),
  `Total reach` = cm(t$total_depth2_reach),
  `Post-notice direct` = cm(t$post_notice_direct_citers),
  check.names = FALSE))
```

![**Figure 3. Distribution of seed-level depth-2 reach.** The x-axis is log-scaled and adds 1 so zero-reach seeds remain visible.](figures/manuscript_fig4_reach_distribution.png)

![**Figure 4. Largest seed-level citation neighborhoods**, split into direct citers and depth-2 descendants.](figures/manuscript_fig5_top_seed_reach.png)

### The Depth-2 Layer Dominates the Edge Count

```{r edge-table}
ed <- aggregate(edge_count ~ depth, s$edge_depths, sum)
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  `Edge depth` = ed$depth,
  `Edge count` = cm(ed$edge_count),
  check.names = FALSE))
```

Direct seed citations account for `r cm(s$depth1_edges)` edges, while
second-layer citation edges account for `r cm(s$depth2_edges)` edges. This
imbalance explains why partial expansion of depth-1 parents would substantially
understate the downstream network.

![**Figure 5. Citation edge counts by graph layer.**](figures/manuscript_fig3_edge_depths.png)

### Citation After Public Notice Remains Common

Post-notice figures are descriptive counts based on available notice dates and
citing-work publication dates; they do not estimate whether the notice changed
citation behavior. To avoid double counting, node-level totals below are counts
of **distinct** papers. Because a citing paper can cite several seeds, we also
report the per-seed relationship sums, which are larger than the distinct-node
counts.

| Measure | Value |
|---|---:|
| Seeds with at least one post-notice direct citer | `r cm(pn$seeds_with_post_direct)` |
| Seeds with at least one post-notice depth-2 descendant | `r cm(pn$seeds_with_post_depth2)` |
| Distinct papers that cite a seed after its notice (direct) | `r cm(pn$direct_unique_nodes)` |
| Distinct depth-2 papers published after a source seed's notice | `r cm(pn$depth2_unique_nodes)` |
| Post-notice seed-direct citation relationships (sum over seeds) | `r cm(pn$direct_relationship_sum)` |
| Post-notice seed-depth-2 relationships (sum over seeds) | `r cm(pn$depth2_relationship_sum)` |

`r cm(pn$direct_unique_nodes)` distinct papers cited a seed after its notice
date, affecting `r cm(pn$seeds_with_post_direct)` of `r cm(r$seed_papers)` seeds
(`r pct(seeds_affected_pct)`). The per-seed relationship sum
(`r cm(pn$direct_relationship_sum)`) is larger because some citing papers cite
several retracted seeds. The depth-2 relationship sum
(`r cm(pn$depth2_relationship_sum)`) exceeds the number of depth-2 nodes and must
be read as a sum of per-seed counts, not as a distinct-paper total; the distinct
depth-2 count is `r cm(pn$depth2_unique_nodes)`.

![**Figure 6. Direct citations of seed papers relative to the notice year.** Each seed's direct citations are aligned so that year 0 is the notice year; bars to the right of the dashed line are citations published after the notice.](figures/manuscript_fig6_post_notice_eventstudy.png)

### Matched Non-Retracted Control Comparison

To ask whether post-notice citation of retracted work is unusual, each seed
paper with at least one dated direct citer was matched to a non-retracted
control paper of the same publication year and similar citation in-degree.
Controls were drawn from the depth-1 layer, the only papers in this
OpenCitations-only graph with a complete, dated incoming-citation history. Each
control was assigned its matched seed's notice date as a pseudo-event, and the
share of each paper's direct citations published after that date was compared
within pairs. This is a conservative comparison: the controls themselves cite a
retracted seed, so they sit in the same citation neighborhoods.

```{r control, results='asis'}
if (!is.null(ctl) && !is.null(ctl$matched_pairs) && ctl$matched_pairs > 0) {
  d <- as.numeric(ctl$median_share_diff)
  sig <- as.numeric(ctl$wilcoxon_p) < 0.05
  direction <- if (!sig) {
    "a similar share of"
  } else if (d > 0) {
    "a **larger** share of"
  } else {
    "a **smaller** share of"
  }
  interp <- if (!sig) {
    paste("The difference is not statistically distinguishable, so this",
          "descriptive comparison does not show retracted papers being cited",
          "post-notice at a rate different from matched controls.")
  } else if (d > 0) {
    paste("Retracted and concerning papers therefore accrue a larger share of",
          "their citations after the notice than comparable non-retracted",
          "papers, consistent with citation that does not respond to the notice.")
  } else {
    paste("Retracted and concerning papers therefore accrue a smaller share of",
          "their citations after the notice than comparable non-retracted",
          "papers.")
  }
  cat(sprintf(paste0(
    "Across %s matched pairs (%s unique control papers), the median post-notice ",
    "citation share was %s for retracted or concerning seeds versus %s for matched ",
    "non-retracted controls, a median paired difference of %s (seeds higher in %s ",
    "of pairs; Wilcoxon signed-rank p %s). Seeds accrue %s their citations after the ",
    "notice relative to matched controls. %s\n"),
    cm(ctl$matched_pairs), cm(ctl$unique_controls),
    sh(ctl$seed_share_median), sh(ctl$control_share_median),
    sh(d),
    paste0(formatC(ctl$pct_pairs_seed_higher, digits = 0, format = "f"), "%"),
    pv(ctl$wilcoxon_p), direction, interp))
} else {
  cat("_Matched-control comparison was not run for this output._\n")
}
```

![**Figure 7. Matched non-retracted control comparison.** (A) Direct citations by year relative to the notice, as a share of each group's citations, for seeds and matched controls. (B) Within-pair difference in the share of citations published after the notice date (seed minus control); the blue line is the median.](figures/manuscript_fig7_control_comparison.png)

### Bridge Identifiers Highlight High-Volume Intermediates

Bridge identifiers are depth-1 nodes that cite a seed and are themselves cited by
many depth-2 nodes. In the OpenCitations-only output many bridge identifiers lack
titles because metadata enrichment was not run, so the bridge table is useful for
prioritizing follow-up rather than for title-level interpretation.

```{r bridge-table}
b <- s$bridge_top10
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  Rank = seq_len(nrow(b)),
  Identifier = b$openalex_id,
  `Cited seed count` = cm(b$cited_seed_count),
  `Depth-2 citer count` = cm(b$depth2_citer_count),
  check.names = FALSE))
```

### Visualizing a Pollution Neighborhood

The full graph has `r cm(s$total_nodes)` nodes and `r cm(s$total_edges)` edges
and cannot be drawn legibly; a force-directed image of 31.6 million edges is an
uninterpretable mass, which is why the complete graph is supplied as compressed
GraphML (see Data Availability). To show what a neighborhood looks like, Figure 8
renders one concrete example: the highest-reach seed, a random sample of its
direct citers, and a random sample of the depth-2 papers citing those citers,
laid out with a force-directed algorithm and colored by depth.

```{r network, results='asis'}
if (!is.null(s$network) && !is.null(s$network$nodes) && s$network$nodes > 0) {
  cat(sprintf(paste0(
    "The example shows the seed *%s* with %s sampled direct citers and %s ",
    "sampled depth-2 descendants (%s nodes drawn). The layered fan from the ",
    "central seed outward illustrates how a single retracted paper anchors a ",
    "large downstream neighborhood.\n"),
    substr(s$network$seed_title, 1, 70), cm(s$network$direct_shown),
    cm(s$network$depth2_shown), cm(s$network$nodes)))
}
```

![**Figure 8. An example citation-pollution neighborhood.** A seeded random sample of the largest seed's ego-network through depth 2. The central red node is the retracted seed, orange nodes are direct citers, and blue nodes are depth-2 descendants. This is one sampled example, not the full graph.](figures/manuscript_fig8_example_network.png)

## Discussion

The completed depth-2 graph shows that downstream citation exposure to retracted
and concerning papers is both widespread and highly concentrated. The median seed
paper had only `r cm(r$reach_median)` unique descendants through depth 2, but the
upper tail was extreme (maximum `r cm(r$reach_max)`), and reach is very unequally
distributed (Gini `r formatC(r$reach_gini, digits = 3, format = "f")`). The most
important empirical feature is therefore not the average, but the skew: a small
number of seeds account for most of the downstream reach, while about a quarter
have no indexed downstream citations at all.

The depth-2 layer dominates the graph: direct citations to seeds accounted for
`r cm(s$depth1_edges)` edges, while citations from terminal depth-2 descendants
accounted for `r cm(s$depth2_edges)` edges. A direct-citation analysis would
therefore miss most of the observed downstream network. This does not mean every
second-order citation transmits the original error or claim; it shows that papers
connected to retracted or concerning work can sit inside very large downstream
citation neighborhoods.

Citation after public notice remained common: `r cm(pn$direct_unique_nodes)`
distinct papers cited a seed after its notice date. These counts should be
interpreted cautiously. A post-notice citation can be critical, neutral, or
unaware of the notice, and this study does not classify citation sentiment or
context, nor does it model a citation rate or compare against a matched
non-retracted control. The event-study view (Figure 6) aligns each seed's direct
citations to its notice year and is descriptive: it shows that citation does not
stop at the notice, not that the notice caused or failed to cause any change.

## Limitations

1. **Descriptive only.** These are network counts and matched descriptive
   comparisons, not causal estimates. The event study is a within-seed alignment
   and the matched-control comparison assigns controls a pseudo-notice date; the
   study does not model time-at-risk, citation sentiment, or a
   difference-in-differences effect of the notice.
2. **Matched-control pool.** Controls are non-retracted papers drawn from the
   depth-1 layer (papers that themselves cite a retracted seed), the only papers
   with a fully crawled incoming-citation history in this OpenCitations-only
   graph. They are not a random sample of the literature, and the difference in
   post-notice citation share may partly reflect the distinct citation-timing
   profiles of high-profile retracted papers rather than the notice alone.
   Matching controls for publication year and citation in-degree, not for field
   or citation burstiness.
2. **OpenCitations-only scope.** OpenAlex expansion was not used, so coverage is
   bounded by the OpenCitations Index; `r cm(r$zero_reach_n)`
   (`r pct(r$zero_reach_pct)`) seeds have zero indexed reach.
3. **No-DOI exclusion.** `r cm(s$no_doi_seeds)` Retraction Watch records without
   a resolvable DOI were excluded. These skew toward older, non-journal, or
   lower-resource literature, so the sample under-represents exactly the work
   most likely to be poorly indexed; reported reach is a lower bound.
4. **Global-depth attribution.** Per-seed reach uses the globally depth-labeled
   graph, so a node shared between seeds is attributed to its nearest seed. This
   is consistent across the study but means per-seed depth-2 reach is a
   global-neighborhood count, not an independent ego-network.
5. **Publication-date coverage.** `r cm(s$depth1_pubdate_null)` depth-1 and
   `r cm(s$depth2_pubdate_null)` depth-2 nodes lack a publication date and cannot
   be classified as post-notice, so post-notice counts are slight lower bounds. A
   small number of source records carry implausible future publication years and
   were not separately filtered.
6. **Overcount parents.** `r cm(rc$overcount_parents)` parents were not
   re-fetched; the bounded excess (`r cm(rc$overcount_extra_citations)` citations)
   is under 0.1% of the graph.
7. **Metadata enrichment is incomplete.** Topic labels and bridge-paper titles
   are missing in this OpenCitations-only output and should be enriched before
   topic-level or title-level claims.
8. **No depth-3 expansion.** The study is complete for depth 2 only.

## Data Availability and Reproducibility

Every headline number and figure in this manuscript is generated from the study
store and the analysis tables by committed code (`R/manuscript.R` and
`inst/manuscript/manuscript.qmd`); the computed values are also written to
`manuscript_stats.json`. Primary analysis tables are in
`outputs/opencitations/tables/`, the full induced graph is exported as compressed
GraphML in `outputs/opencitations/graphs/`, and the rendered manuscript is
available as HTML, PDF, DOCX, and Markdown.

## Conclusion

The study is complete for its stated OpenCitations depth-2 scope. It provides a
full graph artifact, reproducible manuscript figures, validation evidence, and
descriptive results showing that the downstream citation neighborhoods of
retracted and concerning papers are highly skewed and highly concentrated, and
that citation after public notice remains common at scale.

_Generated `r s$generated_at`._
